title: Pipeline sandbox creation with early batch execution
description:
## Directive IMPORTANT: Before doing ANY git or VCS operations, you MUST activate the jujutsu skill by running /jujutsu. This is a jujutsu-managed repository. Using raw git commands will corrupt data. When this bead is complete, mark the final revision with a branch: danver/pipeline-sandbox-creation ## Problem The orchestrator creates all sandboxes upfront before beginning any test execution. With 200 sandboxes, this takes 8.3 seconds. During this time, zero tests are running. Since the LPT scheduler sorts batches heaviest-first, the longest batches (55-67s acceptance tests) are delayed by the full sandbox creation time. ## Evidence from Trace Analysis From Run 3 (200 sandboxes, 525 batches): - Sandbox pool creation: 8.34s - This is now the LARGEST remaining setup phase (after overlapped discovery was implemented) - The first batch does not start until 14.9s into the run - The longest batch (67.6s) starts at ~15s and finishes at ~83s - If it had started at ~7s instead, it would finish at ~75s, saving ~8s off the critical path The sandbox creation happens in `src/orchestrator/pool.rs` lines 40-60: ```rust pub async fn populate

( &mut self, count: usize, provider: &P, config: &SandboxConfig, ) -> Result<(), ProviderError> where P: SandboxProvider, { let futures: Vec<_> = (0..count) .map(|i| { ... provider.create_sandbox(&cfg).await }) .collect(); let sandboxes = futures::future::try_join_all(futures).await?; self.sandboxes.extend(sandboxes); Ok(()) } ``` And in `src/orchestrator.rs`, `run_with_tests` takes an already-populated `SandboxPool` (line 165). The pool is fully populated before `run_with_tests` is called (in `src/main.rs`). ## Required Changes ### 1. Investigate the current flow in main.rs Read `src/main.rs` to understand where `SandboxPool::populate` is called relative to `orchestrator.run_with_tests`. Map the full sequence: 1. Where is the pool created? 2. Where is it populated? 3. Where is it passed to the orchestrator? 4. What happens between populate and run_with_tests? ### 2. Design the pipelining approach The goal is to start executing batches on early-ready sandboxes while remaining sandboxes are still being created. Two approaches: **Option A: Streaming sandbox creation into the queue** - Modify `run_with_tests` to accept sandboxes via a channel instead of a pre-populated pool - Launch sandbox creation in the background, sending each sandbox to the channel as it becomes ready - Workers pull from the channel as sandboxes arrive **Option B: Two-phase pool population** - Create a small initial pool (e.g., 10-20 sandboxes) synchronously - Start execution with the initial pool - Continue creating remaining sandboxes in the background, adding them to the pool as they become ready - Workers check for new sandboxes periodically **Option C: Overlap pool creation with scheduling** - Run `populate` concurrently with duration loading + scheduling (which takes ~0.3s) - This is a minimal change but only saves ~0.3s Choose whichever option best fits the existing architecture. Option A is most elegant but requires the most refactoring. Option B is a good middle ground. Option C is low-effort but low-impact. ### 3. Implement the chosen approach Implement with proper error handling. If a sandbox fails to create mid-execution, existing running sandboxes should continue. The orchestrator should work with fewer sandboxes than requested (just slower). ### 4. Add tests - Test that execution starts before all sandboxes are ready - Test that the system works correctly with fewer sandboxes than requested (partial creation failure) - Test that all batches complete even if sandbox creation is slow ### 5. Preserve trace events The current trace emits `sandbox_pool_create` as a single span. With pipelining, this span should either: - Cover the full creation time (from first to last sandbox) - Or be replaced with per-sandbox creation events ## Expected Impact - Saves 5-8s off the critical path by overlapping sandbox creation with early batch execution - The heaviest batches (acceptance tests at 55-67s) start sooner, reducing total wall clock - Improvement is proportional to sandbox count (more sandboxes = more savings) ## Files to Modify - src/main.rs (understand current flow, possibly restructure) - src/orchestrator.rs (modify run_with_tests to accept streaming sandboxes) - src/orchestrator/pool.rs (possibly add streaming creation method)

title: Investigate and instrument slow acceptance tests for optimization
description:
## Directive

IMPORTANT: Before doing ANY git or VCS operations, you MUST activate the jujutsu skill by running /jujutsu. This is a jujutsu-managed repository. Using raw git commands will corrupt data.

When this bead is complete, mark the final revision with a branch: danver/investigate-slow-acceptance-tests

## Problem

Five acceptance tests individually take 47-68 seconds each, forming the hard floor on execution time. No amount of parallelism or scheduling improvement can reduce the wall clock below the duration of the slowest single test. These tests are the dominant bottleneck in Run 3.

## Evidence from Trace Analysis

From Run 3 (26,210 tests, 200 sandboxes):

| Batch | Tests | Duration | Sandbox |
|-------|-------|----------|---------|
| batch_3 | 1 | 67.6s | 17 |
| batch_1 | 1 | 58.1s | 2 |
| batch_0 | 1 | 48.0s | 0 |
| batch_4 | 1 | 48.0s | 32 |
| batch_2 | 1 | 47.6s | 3 |

These 5 tests consume 269 sandbox-seconds. The longest (67.6s) is 72% of the entire 93.8s execution window. Even a 2x improvement on just the slowest test would save ~34s off the critical path.

## Context

These tests are in the `mng` repository (the repo that uses offload to run its tests), not in the offload repository itself. The offload tool runs whatever tests it discovers -- it does not control their content. However, we can instrument offload to help identify what makes these tests slow.

## Required Changes

### 1. Add per-test timing to batch output

Currently, offload knows the total batch duration but not individual test durations within a batch. For single-test batches this is fine, but for multi-test batches the per-test breakdown is invisible.

In `src/orchestrator/runner.rs`, after downloading the JUnit XML results, parse the `time` attribute from each `<testcase>` element and log the top-N slowest tests:

```rust
// After downloading junit.xml, log slowest tests
let mut test_times: Vec<(&str, f64)> = Vec::new();
// Parse <testcase name="..." time="..."> elements
// Sort by time descending
// Log top 5 slowest
for (name, time) in test_times.iter().take(5) {
    info!("[SLOW TEST] {}: {:.1}s", name, time);
}
```

### 2. Add a `--slow-test-threshold` CLI flag

Add a `--slow-test-threshold` flag (default: 30s) that causes offload to emit a warning for any test exceeding the threshold:

```
WARNING: Test 'test_full_acceptance_flow' took 67.6s (threshold: 30s)
```

This makes slow tests visible in CI output without requiring trace analysis.

### 3. Add slow test data to the Perfetto trace

In the trace output, add per-test duration events. Currently the trace has batch-level events (`exec_batch`, `download_results`). Add individual test events within the exec thread:

```rust
// For each testcase in the junit XML:
tracer.complete_event(
    test_name,
    "test",
    sandbox_pid,
    TID_EXEC,
    test_start_us,
    test_duration_us,
);
```

This requires parsing the JUnit XML for individual test times and mapping them back to the trace timeline. The start time can be approximated (batch_start + cumulative_previous_test_times).

### 4. Add a summary section to the run output

After the existing summary (passed/failed/flaky counts), add a "Slowest Tests" section:

```
Slowest tests:
  1. test_full_acceptance_flow  67.6s
  2. test_end_to_end_pipeline   58.1s
  3. test_modal_integration     48.0s
  ...
```

Use the JUnit XML `time` attributes as the source of truth.

### 5. Write tests

- Test that the slow test warning is emitted when a test exceeds the threshold
- Test that the slow test summary is correctly sorted and limited to top N
- Test that per-test trace events are emitted correctly

## Expected Impact

- No direct wall-clock improvement (this is instrumentation)
- Enables the mng team to identify and profile the specific slow tests
- The slow test warnings in CI output will create visibility and pressure to fix them
- Per-test trace events enable deeper analysis in Perfetto UI

## Files to Modify
- src/orchestrator/runner.rs (add per-test timing extraction from JUnit XML)
- src/main.rs (add --slow-test-threshold flag)
- src/report.rs or src/report/junit.rs (add slow test summary to output)
- src/trace.rs (possibly add per-test trace events)

comments.author: danver
comments.text:
Cancelled by Coordinator on 2026-05-19 at user direction after value-based analysis.

The bead's data surfaces are largely already in offload or in the test framework layer:

- src/report/junit.rs:579-589 (load_test_durations) already logs the top-10 slowest tests at INFO level at the start of every run that has a prior junit.xml. Same data the bead's sub-deliverable #1 proposes to log per batch.
- offload logs (per README) already exposes per-test results from JUnit XML, including duration via the time attribute.
- The pytest framework has --durations=N; nextest has slow-timeout; vitest has similar. These are established idioms at the framework layer where execution actually happens. Reinventing this in offload duplicates effort and creates inconsistency across frameworks.

The motivating 5 tests are all single-test batches (batch_size=1), for which batch duration equals test duration. The existing per-batch trace events already give a per-test view of those specific tests, making sub-deliverable #3 (per-test Perfetto events) useful for multi-test batches but moot for the bead's own evidence.

The strongest single piece would have been #4 (Slowest Tests section in print_summary) — net-new and creates organizational pressure via terminal output — but on its own it does not justify a new bead given the other surfaces. The Mng team, which owns the slow tests, already has multiple paths to the same data.

If a future change to JUnit reporting requires touching this area, the print_summary "Slowest tests" section is a reasonable thing to add opportunistically.

title: Refactor provider lifecycle: split from_config() into lightweight constructor + prepare()
description:
## Task
Split the heavy async `from_config()` on Modal and Default providers into:
1. A lightweight, synchronous `from_config()` that only stores config and connector
2. An explicit `prepare()` trait method that runs the image build and returns image_id

This is a pure refactor. Existing `offload run` behavior must be unchanged.

## Details
See `plans/checkpoint-images.md` section "Prior Refactors" for the full specification.

## Key files to read
- `src/provider.rs` — SandboxProvider trait
- `src/provider/modal.rs` — ModalProvider::from_config() (the heavy constructor to split)
- `src/provider/default.rs` — DefaultProvider::from_config() (same split)
- `src/provider/local.rs` — LocalProvider (may need prepare() stub)
- `src/main.rs` — call sites of from_config() that need to call prepare() after

## Acceptance criteria
- `from_config()` is synchronous and lightweight on all providers
- `prepare()` is a new trait method on SandboxProvider
- All existing call sites updated: from_config() then prepare()
- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy` passes (no warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` passes
- No behavior change in offload run

1. Always include the exit code in the error message.
2. If stderr is empty, include stdout (trimmed, capped at 500 chars) as fallback.
3. If both are empty, say 'no output captured'.

Files to change:
- src/framework/default.rs lines 93-97
- src/framework/pytest.rs lines 127-131
- src/framework/cargo.rs lines 149-153
- src/framework/vitest.rs lines 197-201 (already has exit code, but needs stdout fallback)

Pattern for each: format the error as:
  '{framework} discovery failed (exit {status}): {detail}'
where detail = stderr.trim() if non-empty, else stdout.trim() (capped at 500 chars) if non-empty, else 'no output captured'.

title: Batch-scope artifact collection: find scans whole sandbox tree, leaking prior batches files
description:
ARTIFACT COLLECTION IS NOT BATCH-SCOPED (related to code-q9wa, same bug class).

PROBLEM
try_download_artifacts in src/orchestrator/runner.rs collects artifacts with
build_find_command, producing "find . -type f -path <glob>" (runner.rs ~114-139,
~625). This scans the entire sandbox working tree for glob matches with NO time
filter and NO batch scoping. A sandbox is reused across many batches sequentially
(spawn.rs worker loop; sandbox reused via into_sandbox()). Nothing removes or
isolates artifact files between batches.

CONSEQUENCE
For a sandbox sb-0 running batch K then batch K+1:
- Batch K tests write artifact files into the tree; find tars them into
  {output_dir}/{safe_id}/{K}/.
- Batch K+1 tests run on the SAME filesystem; batch K files are still present.
- Batch K+1 find collects batch K leftovers PLUS batch K+1 new files, into
  {output_dir}/{safe_id}/{K+1}/.
So {safe_id}/{K+1}/ is labeled batch K+1 but holds a SUPERSET: every artifact from
every earlier batch on that sandbox. Harms:
  1. Mislabeled / contaminated artifact bundles (debugging surfaces unrelated files).
  2. Quadratic re-download: each batch re-tars and re-downloads the growing set
     (about S*M(M+1)/2 transferred for M batches per sandbox instead of S*M).
download_globs_failure_only makes a late failing batch dump the whole history.

NOT THE SAME AS code-q9wa
The artifact PATHS are already batch-indexed (remote tar
/tmp/offload-artifacts-{safe_id}-{batch_idx}.tar.gz; extraction dir
{output_dir}/{safe_id}/{batch_idx}/; batch_idx is globally unique). This is a
file-COLLECTION-SCOPE bug, not a path collision. Batch-indexing the path does
not fix it.

SEVERITY
Artifacts are best-effort (not a test-result correctness bug), but this is a real
data-integrity (mislabeled bundles) and efficiency (quadratic transfer) defect.

FIX DIRECTION
Touch a marker file (e.g. /tmp/offload-batch-{batch_idx}) immediately before the
batch test command runs, then collect with "find . -newer <marker> ...", so only
files this batch produced or modified are captured. Do NOT delete matched files
after taring -- globs can match tracked source files.

TESTS
Add a regression test for the batch-scoped find command construction.

DEFINITION OF DONE
cargo fmt --check; cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features (no warnings);
cargo nextest run (within TESTING.md timeout); ratchets check.

title: Investigate Modal image ID caching quirk - image_id may not be final
description:
Modal has a known quirk where the image_id returned from build may not be the 'final' image ID. This causes cached images to still require some rebuild time. Investigate:
1. How Modal image IDs work (im-* format)
2. What happens when we call image.build(app) - is the returned ID interim?
3. Whether there's a way to get the truly final/hydrated image ID
4. What changes are needed to modal_sandbox.py and the Rust cache to store the correct ID

## Investigation Results

**Root Cause:** Modal images have two phases - lazy (local shell) and hydrated (fully resolved on server). When we get `object_id` after `.build()` without explicit `.hydrate()`, we may get an interim ID that isn't fully resolved on Modal's backend.

**Solution:** Add explicit `.hydrate()` calls:
1. After `image.build(app)`, call `image.hydrate()` before reading `object_id`
2. When reconstructing with `Image.from_id(cached_id)`, call `.hydrate()` before use

**Key findings:**
- `.build(app)` triggers build but may not fully resolve the image
- `.hydrate()` explicitly forces full hydration on Modal's servers
- `.from_id()` is lazy - returns local shell until hydrated
- Only after hydration is `object_id` guaranteed to be the final ID

title: Add .hydrate() calls to Modal image builds for proper caching
description:
Based on investigation in code-34, Modal images need explicit hydration to get the final image ID.

## Changes Required

### In scripts/modal_sandbox.py:

1. **All build functions** (build_dockerfile, build_preset, _build_default, _build_rust, _build_sculptor, etc.):
   After `image.build(app)`, add `image.hydrate()` before printing `object_id`:
   ```python
   image.build(app)
   image.hydrate()
   print(image.object_id)
   ```

2. **create_with_image function**:
   After `Image.from_id()`, hydrate before using in Sandbox:
   ```python
   image = modal.Image.from_id(image_id)
   image.hydrate()
   sandbox = modal.Sandbox.create(app=app, image=image, ...)
   ```

## IMPORTANT: Style Constraints
- **NO trailing comments** - the ratchet budget for `no-trailing-comments [scripts]` is 3
- Do NOT add comments on the same line as code
- If explanation is needed, add a comment on a SEPARATE LINE above the code
- Prefer no comments at all - `.hydrate()` is self-explanatory

## Why This Fixes the Issue
- `.build()` triggers the build but may return before full registration
- `.hydrate()` ensures the image is completely resolved on Modal's backend
- `.from_id()` is lazy - without hydration, it needs server resolution at use time
- With proper hydration, cached IDs will be truly 'final' and reuse will be instant

title: Fix strip_prefix(1) incorrectly stripping real path component on rename/copy in apply-diff
Fix Modal image hydration - cannot hydrate on demand
description:
The .hydrate() call fails with:
```
raise ExecutionError(
    "Images cannot currently be hydrated on demand; you can build an Image by running an App that uses it."
)
```

## Investigation Results
Modal images can only be hydrated by **using** them in an App (e.g., Sandbox.create()).
`.hydrate()` cannot be called on images directly.

## Solution Options
1. **Remove .hydrate() calls** - Accept slight overhead on reuse
2. **Create dummy sandbox** - Force hydration by briefly creating a sandbox:
   ```python
   image.build(app)
   temp_sandbox = modal.Sandbox.create(app=app, image=image, timeout=10)
   temp_sandbox.terminate()
   print(image.object_id)  # Now fully hydrated
   ```

## Chosen Approach
Per user suggestion, try Option 2: Create a minimal dummy sandbox to force hydration, then immediately terminate it. This ensures the cached image ID is truly final.

## Changes Required
Replace `.hydrate()` calls with dummy sandbox creation pattern in all build_* functions in scripts/modal_sandbox.py

title: Investigate 30-second delay between cache hit and image loading
description:
## Problem
There's a ~30 second pause between cache lookup and actual image loading:
```
19:54:14 INFO Using cached Modal image for dockerfile:.devcontainer/Dockerfile: im-YlDkL514ar5gcMsBUhUBTd
19:54:46 INFO Loading image im-YlDkL514ar5gcMsBUhUBTd...  <-- 32 seconds later!
```

## Investigation Results

### Code Flow
1. Rust: `get_or_build_image()` finds cached entry, logs 'Using cached Modal image', returns image_id immediately
2. Rust: `call_python_create_with_image()` spawns Python process
3. Python: `modal.App.lookup(app_name, create_if_missing=True)` - possibly slow?
4. Python: `modal.Image.from_id(image_id)` - THIS IS THE DELAY
5. Python: `modal.Sandbox.create()`

### Root Cause
The delay is in the Python script between receiving the image_id and creating the sandbox. Likely causes:
1. `modal.App.lookup()` may be doing network calls
2. `modal.Image.from_id()` needs to resolve/hydrate the image from Modal's servers
3. Python process startup overhead (uv, imports)

### Possible Solutions
1. **Add more logging** to pinpoint exactly which call is slow
2. **Pre-create the App** during image build, not sandbox creation
3. **Accept the latency** if it's inherent to Modal's API design
4. **Reuse Python process** instead of spawning new one each time (complex)

## Next Step
Add detailed timing logs to modal_sandbox.py to identify which specific call takes 30 seconds.

comments.author: danver
comments.text:
Cancelled by Coordinator on 2026-05-19 at user direction after value-based analysis.

The investigation has decayed since being filed 2026-01-30. The bead's specified Next Step ('add detailed timing logs to modal_sandbox.py to identify which specific call takes 30 seconds') is already done: scripts/modal_sandbox.py lines 628, 646, 656, 663, 692 emit per-sub-phase timings at DEBUG level, and the Python logger is unconditionally DEBUG. A single 'offload run --verbose -c offload-cargo-modal.toml' would surface those timings.

The bead's cited evidence references log strings and functions that no longer exist ("Using cached Modal image", get_or_build_image, call_python_create_with_image). The image-cache architecture moved into src/image_cache.rs with a different call path (prepare_with_prewarm, try_thin_diff). The 4-month-old trace would not reproduce against current code without a fresh run.

The three suspect sub-phases (Python subprocess startup, modal.App.lookup, modal.Image.from_id) all happen per-create-call in a fresh process; modal.App.lookup is duplicated in prepare and create (lines 442, 643) without shared state. The only structural fix is daemonizing modal_sandbox.py — a long-lived Python process with RPC, which subsumes this bead and code-100 and is a substantially larger unit of work than the bead's scope.

If the 30s gap still matters, file a fresh bead for daemonization rather than reopening this one.

title: Implement tar-based file transfer for Modal sandboxes
description:
Optimize Modal sandbox file uploads by using tar for file delivery.

## Background
Running tests via Modal is currently slow due to time spent uploading multiple files to the target. Each file is transferred individually via sandbox.open(), which is inefficient for large directory trees.

## Solution
1. On the client side: Create a tar archive of the directory to transfer
2. Transfer the single tar file to the sandbox
3. On the sandbox side: Extract the tar archive to the destination directory

## Implementation locations
- scripts/modal_sandbox.py: Update copy_dir_to_sandbox() function to use tar-based approach
- The tar module is already a dependency in Cargo.toml

## Expected improvements
- Reduced number of file operations (one tar vs many individual files)
- Faster transfer times due to fewer round trips
- Better handling of directory structures

## Files to modify
- scripts/modal_sandbox.py: copy_dir_to_sandbox() function

@click.option('--env', 'env_vars', multiple=True, help='Environment variable (KEY=VALUE)')
def create_from_image(image_id, copy_dirs, env_vars):

title: Add max_batch_size constraint to LPT scheduler
description:
## Directive

IMPORTANT: Before doing ANY git or VCS operations, you MUST activate the jujutsu skill by running /jujutsu. This is a jujutsu-managed repository. Using raw git commands will corrupt data.

When this bead is complete, mark the final revision with a branch: danver/cap-batch-size-lpt

## Problem

The LPT scheduler in src/orchestrator/scheduler.rs produces "mega-batches" of 300+ tests. The `would_fit` method (line 54-63) only checks two constraints: total command string length (`MAX_BATCH_COMMAND_LEN = 30,000`) and an optional duration cap (`max_batch_duration`). There is no constraint on the number of tests per batch.

When hundreds of fast tests each have estimated durations of ~0.01s, the scheduler packs 300+ of them into a single batch because their summed durations stay under the 10-second `MAX_BATCH_DURATION` cap. These mega-batches then take 14-35 seconds in practice (4-10x the estimate), because per-test overhead (fixture setup, import time, etc.) is not captured in historical durations.

## Evidence from Trace Analysis

From Run 3 (26,210 tests, 200 sandboxes, 525 batches):
- Max batch size: 318 tests
- Batches 500-524 each contain 314-318 tests
- These mega-batches take 20-26s actual (vs ~3s estimated)
- The estimation error is the root cause of scheduling imbalance

## Required Changes

### 1. Add `max_batch_size` parameter to `schedule_lpt`

In `src/orchestrator/scheduler.rs`:

Add a new parameter `max_batch_size: Option<usize>` to `schedule_lpt` (line 184). Update the `would_fit` method (line 54-63) to also check:

```rust
fn would_fit(
    &self,
    test_id_len: usize,
    duration: Duration,
    max_batch_duration: Option<Duration>,
    max_batch_size: Option<usize>,
) -> bool {
    self.is_empty()
        || (self.command_len + test_id_len <= MAX_BATCH_COMMAND_LEN
            && max_batch_duration.is_none_or(|cap| self.load + duration <= cap)
            && max_batch_size.is_none_or(|max| self.tests.len() < max))
}
```

### 2. Add `max_batch_size` to configuration

In `src/config/schema.rs`, add `max_batch_size: Option<usize>` to `OffloadConfig` (or whichever struct holds `max_parallel` and `max_batch_duration`). Use `#[serde(default)]` so existing configs work without it.

### 3. Wire it through the orchestrator

In `src/orchestrator.rs`, the call to `scheduler.schedule_lpt` (around line 263) currently passes `Some(MAX_BATCH_DURATION)`. Update this to also pass `max_batch_size` from the config.

### 4. Set a sensible default

When `max_batch_size` is None (not configured), use a default of 50. This is based on trace analysis showing that batches above ~50 tests consistently have >3x estimation error. The default should be a constant like:

```rust
const DEFAULT_MAX_BATCH_SIZE: usize = 50;
```

### 5. Add tests

Add the following test cases to the existing `mod tests` in `src/orchestrator/scheduler.rs`:

- `test_schedule_lpt_respects_max_batch_size`: Create 100 tests with 0.01s durations and max_batch_size=50. Verify no batch exceeds 50 tests.
- `test_schedule_lpt_max_batch_size_creates_extra_batches`: With max_parallel=2 and 200 tests with max_batch_size=50, verify at least 4 batches are created (200/50).
- `test_schedule_lpt_single_test_ignores_batch_size`: A single test that exceeds duration cap still gets its own batch regardless of batch size setting.
- `test_schedule_lpt_batch_size_and_duration_both_enforced`: With both max_batch_duration and max_batch_size set, verify both constraints hold.

### 6. Update existing tests

The existing `schedule_lpt` tests pass `None` for `max_batch_duration` in many cases. Update them to also pass `None` for `max_batch_size` so they continue working.

## Expected Impact

Based on trace analysis, capping at 50 tests per batch should:
- Reduce max batch duration from ~26s to ~8-10s
- Improve tail latency by 10-20s
- Better match estimated vs actual durations

## Files to Modify
- src/orchestrator/scheduler.rs (primary: add max_batch_size to would_fit and schedule_lpt)
- src/config/schema.rs (add max_batch_size config field)
- src/orchestrator.rs (wire config to scheduler call)

comments.author: danver
comments.text:
Cancelled by Coordinator on 2026-05-19 at user direction after value-based analysis.

The bead's diagnosis (per-test overhead is missing from the duration model; mega-batches of 318 fast tests take ~26s actual vs ~3s estimated) is correct, but the proposed mechanism (hard max_batch_size cap, default 50) is a workaround tuned to one trace, not a root-cause fix.

Specific issues:
1. The bead's Required Changes section references APIs that no longer exist: would_fit currently takes only test_id_len (no max_batch_duration parameter); there is no schedule_lpt function (scheduling happens in Scheduler::new); there is no MAX_BATCH_DURATION constant. The bead would need a rewrite before any Coding agent could act on it.
2. The 50-test cap is derived from N=1 (the Mng workload trace). No reason to expect it generalizes.
3. The asserted 10-20s tail-latency improvement competes with code-101: per code-101, a single 67s acceptance test sets the wall-clock floor for the same Run 3. Capping mega-batches reclaims idle sandbox-seconds (cost), not wall clock, unless the workload mix changes.
4. Splitting 525 batches into ~700 adds per-batch download/exec overhead (mean 8.9s/batch per code-98's trace), partially eating the savings.
5. No measurement plan — the impact claim is asserted, not testable.

The more principled fix, if revisited later, is to add per-test overhead to the LPT load estimate directly (derived ~70ms/test from the same trace) so the model accounts for what it's currently missing. That generalizes across suites and is roughly the same diff size.

title: Remove debug ls /tmp/ exec from download path and reduce I/O overhead
description:
## Directive

IMPORTANT: Before doing ANY git or VCS operations, you MUST activate the jujutsu skill by running /jujutsu. This is a jujutsu-managed repository. Using raw git commands will corrupt data.

When this bead is complete, mark the final revision with a branch: danver/remove-debug-ls-reduce-io

## Problem

The `try_download_results` method in `src/orchestrator/runner.rs` (lines 474-491) executes a debug `ls -la /tmp/` command inside the sandbox before every single result download. This exec is purely diagnostic logging and adds latency to every batch completion. With 525 batches and 200 concurrent sandboxes, these debug execs collectively consume significant I/O time and contribute to contention.

## Evidence from Trace Analysis

From Run 3 (525 batches, 200 sandboxes):
- Mean download time: 8.9s per batch
- Median download time: 7.9s
- Total cumulative download time: 4,685 sandbox-seconds
- Download overhead averages ~22% of batch duration, extending to 50%+ for some batches
- Run 2 (before overlapped discovery) had mean download of 12.8s -- the 200-sandbox concurrent I/O saturates local filesystem

The debug `ls -la /tmp/` is specifically at lines 474-491 of runner.rs:

```rust
// Debug: List /tmp contents before download
info!("[DOWNLOAD] Sandbox {} listing /tmp/ contents...", sandbox_id);
let list_cmd = crate::provider::Command::new("ls").arg("-la").arg("/tmp/");
if let Ok(mut stream) = self.sandbox.exec_stream(&list_cmd).await {
    use futures::StreamExt;
    let mut tmp_contents = Vec::new();
    while let Some(line) = stream.next().await {
        tmp_contents.push(format!("{:?}", line));
    }
    info!("[DOWNLOAD] Sandbox {} /tmp/ contents: {}",
        sandbox_id, tmp_contents.join(" | "));
}
```

This entire block runs for every batch on every sandbox.

## Required Changes

### 1. Remove the debug `ls -la /tmp/` block

In `src/orchestrator/runner.rs`, delete lines 474-491 (the block starting with `// Debug: List /tmp contents before download` through the closing brace of the `if let Ok(mut stream)` block). Keep the actual download logic that follows.

### 2. Guard remaining diagnostic logging behind debug level

The remaining `info!` calls in `try_download_results` (lines 498-501, 504, etc.) are noisy at INFO level with 525 batches. Downgrade the non-error download logging to `debug!` level:
- `[DOWNLOAD] Sandbox {} downloading {}...` -> debug!
- `[DOWNLOAD] Sandbox {} download succeeded` -> debug!
- `[DOWNLOAD] Sandbox {} junit.xml: ... bytes, ... testcases` -> debug!

Keep ERROR-level logs for actual failures (download failed, empty XML, read failed).

### 3. For LocalSandbox: consider skipping download entirely (investigation)

The `LocalSandbox::download()` implementation copies files from the local filesystem. For the local provider, the result XML files at `/tmp/{sandbox_id}.xml` are already on the host filesystem. Investigate whether the download step can be bypassed for the local provider by reading directly from the sandbox temporary directory.

This is an investigation task -- if it is straightforward to implement, do it. If it requires significant refactoring of the download/result-collection interface, document findings and skip the implementation.

### 4. Add tests

If the debug ls removal changes the behavior of `try_download_results` in any observable way (it should not -- it is purely side-effect logging), add a test confirming download still works correctly.

## Expected Impact

- Removing the debug `ls` exec saves ~0.5-2s per batch (exec + stream collection overhead)
- With 525 batches, estimated total savings: 250-1000 sandbox-seconds
- Reducing log noise makes real errors more visible
- Overall wall-clock savings estimated at 5-15s from reduced I/O contention

## Files to Modify
- src/orchestrator/runner.rs (primary: remove debug ls block, downgrade log levels)
- Possibly src/provider/local.rs (if local-provider download bypass is feasible)

title: Make sandbox cleanup async (do not block wall-clock time)

comments.author: danver
comments.text: Cancelled by Coordinator on 2026-05-19 at user direction. Implementation attempt (uncommitted, then discarded) revealed that fire-and-forget tokio::spawn is the wrong primitive given main is #[tokio::main(flavor = "multi_thread")] and returns shortly after run_with_tests. The runtime drop on main return cancels the detached cleanup task before sandbox.terminate() completes: HTTP-backed providers (Modal, default-via-modal) leave server-side sandboxes alive until idle timeout (billed); local Docker containers leak. The only user-visible benefit of the migration is that print_summary fires ~6s before runtime drop. A future bead, if filed, should pursue the simpler design: keep terminate synchronous but move print_summary above it so the user sees results immediately while cleanup still completes correctly.

title: Add --override-image-id CLI flag to bypass Modal image build
description:
## Goal

Add a per-invocation CLI escape hatch: `offload run --override-image-id <ID>`.
When passed, Offload runs tests against the given pre-built Modal image exactly
as-is and skips its entire image-setup pipeline (Dockerfile build, thin-diff
patch of local changes, checkpoint cache). For users who have already built
their own Modal image and do not want Offload to build one for them.

This is a jury-rigged, invocation-scoped override. It is NOT a config option.

## Hard constraints (do not violate)

- Do NOT add any field to `ModalProviderConfig` or anything else in
  `src/config/schema.rs`. Do NOT add config validation in `src/config.rs`.
  The override is a CLI flag ONLY — nothing in the TOML schema.
- The branch has been reset to a clean base. You are implementing fresh: there
  is NO pre-existing `override_image_id` config field anywhere. Do not look for
  one or try to reuse one.

## Approach

1. CLI flag: add `override_image_id: Option<String>` to the `Commands::Run`
   struct in `src/main.rs` with `#[arg(long)]` (clap renders it as
   `--override-image-id`). Give it a clear `help` string: an escape hatch that
   makes Offload run tests against the given Modal image ID directly, bypassing
   all image build/patch/cache setup.

2. Thread it to `prepare()` via `PrepareContext` (`src/provider.rs`), mirroring
   exactly how the existing `no_cache` CLI flag is threaded. Add an
   `override_image_id` field to `PrepareContext` and populate it in
   `run_prepare()` in `src/main.rs` from the CLI value.

3. `ModalProvider::prepare()` (`src/provider/modal.rs`): when the
   `PrepareContext` override is `Some`, short-circuit — skip
   `prepare_with_prewarm` entirely, set `self.image_id` to the override value,
   return `Ok(Some(value))`. Emit ONE loud `tracing::warn!` stating that the
   configured image build/patch/cache is bypassed for this run and the image is
   used as-is.

4. Non-Modal guard: `--override-image-id` only makes sense for the `modal`
   provider. If it is passed while `config.provider` is not Modal, fail early in
   `run_tests()` with a clear error (e.g. "--override-image-id is only
   supported with the modal provider").

## Tests (TDD — failing test first)

- CLI parsing: `offload run --override-image-id im-x ...` parses and the value
  lands on the `Run` command struct.
- `prepare()` short-circuit: with the `PrepareContext` override set to
  `Some(...)`, `ModalProvider::prepare()` returns the override ID and does NOT
  run a build. Mirror the existing modal.rs prepare-test style.
- Non-Modal guard returns an error when the flag is set with a non-modal
  provider, if reasonably testable at that seam.

## Docs

- The clap `help` string is the primary documentation.
- If README enumerates `offload run` flags, add one line for
  `--override-image-id` matching that list's style. Do NOT touch the
  offload-onboard skill — that skill is about config setup; this is not config.

## Out of scope

- No config/schema changes. No base-commit incremental mode. No registry images.
- No drive-by refactors.

## Definition of Done

- Read README.md, AGENTS.md, STYLE_GUIDE.md, TESTING.md before changing code.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy` (no warnings), `cargo nextest run`, and
  `ratchets check` all pass.
- Prefer a small number of coherent atomic commits (the user explicitly wants a
  clean commit history) — e.g. one feature+tests commit, optionally one docs
  commit. Each commit must typecheck and pass checks.
- Commit messages include the `Co-authored-by: Sculptor <sculptor@imbue.com>`
  trailer.
- Do NOT commit `.beads/` changes — the Coordinator manages bead state.
- Do NOT close the bead — report back to the Coordinator.

title: Update README with history system documentation
Filter .dockerignore-matching paths from generate_checkpoint_diff
description:
## Problem

The thin-diff produced by `generate_checkpoint_diff` (src/git.rs:386-438) is not .dockerignore-aware, while the base image (export_tree + copy_untracked_files) is. This asymmetry means tracked-but-dockerignored files (e.g. offload-history.jsonl) generate Modify hunks against an image that deliberately omits them, causing apply-diff to fail or to ship dockerignored junk to Modal.

## Fix location

src/git.rs, function `generate_checkpoint_diff` (lines 386-438).

After `git add -A` (line 416) and before `git diff --cached --binary` (line 419):
1. Enumerate staged paths in the temp index that match .dockerignore.
2. `git update-index --remove` those paths from the temp index.
3. Then run the diff as before.

## Implementation approach

Use git's own dockerignore filter for consistency with copy_untracked_files() (src/git.rs:326-374), which already uses `--exclude-from=.dockerignore` and accepts the gitignore-style approximation. **Do NOT introduce a new crate dependency** (buildkit-rs-ignore is 0.1.0 / unmaintained; ignore crate is gitignore-flavored anyway).

Suggested approach:
- If `.dockerignore` exists in the repo root, run (against the temp index, via the existing run_git_with_index closure):
  `git ls-files --cached --ignored --exclude-from=.dockerignore -z`
  This lists tracked paths in the index that match .dockerignore patterns. The -z flag uses NUL terminators (paths may contain newlines).
- For each returned path, run `git update-index --force-remove --stdin -z` and pipe the NUL-separated path list via stdin to drop them from the temp index in one call.
- If `.dockerignore` does not exist or is empty, no-op (current behavior preserved).

Note: `git ls-files --ignored` requires --cached or --others; with --cached, it lists files in the index that match the exclude patterns. Verify the exact flag combination during implementation by reading `git ls-files --help`.

## Tests (TDD — write these first, watch them fail, then fix)

Add to the `generate_checkpoint_diff tests` section in src/git.rs (lines 610+):

1. `test_generate_checkpoint_diff_skips_dockerignored_tracked_modification`:
   - init_temp_repo
   - write `.dockerignore` containing `secrets.txt`, commit it
   - write and commit initial `secrets.txt`
   - capture sha = HEAD
   - modify secrets.txt
   - call generate_checkpoint_diff
   - assert: result is None OR patch does not contain "secrets.txt"

2. `test_generate_checkpoint_diff_skips_dockerignored_new_file`:
   - init_temp_repo
   - write `.dockerignore` containing `*.log`, commit it
   - capture sha = HEAD
   - create new untracked `build.log`
   - call generate_checkpoint_diff
   - assert: result is None OR patch does not contain "build.log"

3. `test_generate_checkpoint_diff_no_dockerignore_unchanged`:
   - regression — existing tests must still pass when no .dockerignore exists.

Look at existing tests at src/git.rs:610+ (test_generate_checkpoint_diff_no_changes, test_generate_checkpoint_diff_tracked_modification, test_generate_checkpoint_diff_untracked_file) for the helper patterns (init_temp_repo, git_in).

## Definition of Done
- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy` passes (no warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` passes
- `ratchets check` passes
- New tests pass; existing tests still pass

## Constraints
- Minimal diff: do not refactor surrounding code.
- Do not pull in new crates.
- Do not touch `copy_untracked_files`.
- The fix is symmetric with copy_untracked_files() but isolated to `generate_checkpoint_diff`.
- Patterns: use git's gitignore-style approximation (matches existing approximation in copy_untracked_files). Full .dockerignore semantic fidelity is out of scope.

title: Include batch index in remote JUnit result path to prevent stale-result reads
description:
CONFIRMED correctness bug (verified by investigation subagent).

PROBLEM
src/orchestrator/runner.rs builds the remote JUnit result path as
  format!("/tmp/{}.{}", sandbox_id, framework.report_format())   (runner.rs ~line 318)
keyed by sandbox ID only, with NO batch index. One sandbox runs many batches
sequentially (spawn.rs worker loop ~line 59; sandbox reused via into_sandbox()
~line 294), so every batch on a sandbox writes and reads the SAME
/tmp/{sandbox_id}.{ext}. No framework produce_test_execution_command removes or
truncates that file first (pytest/default/cargo/vitest), and test runners only
flush the JUnit file at session end.

CONSEQUENCE
If a non-first batch on a sandbox exits WITHOUT writing the file (a non-timeout
abnormal exit -- OOM-kill / crash after tests finish but before the JUnit flush;
timeouts are filtered out before download), try_download_results (runner.rs
~516-592) downloads the PREVIOUS batch stale file. Its only integrity gate is a
content.is_empty() check, which a stale non-empty file passes. The stale results
are then attributed to the current batch. With retried or schedule_individual
tests, a prior attempt failure can be silently recorded as the current (passing)
attempt result -- CI fails on green code, with no warning.

FIX
Include batch_idx in the remote result path so each batch gets a unique path,
e.g. /tmp/{sandbox_id}-{batch_idx}.{ext}. This mirrors the pattern already used
by try_download_artifacts for its tar archive
(/tmp/offload-artifacts-{safe_id}-{batch_idx}.tar.gz, runner.rs ~610).
TestRunner already carries self.batch_idx (runner.rs ~98). Keep the diff minimal.

TESTS
Add a regression test proving two batches on the same sandbox use distinct
remote result paths (and thus a stale file cannot be read).

DEFINITION OF DONE
cargo fmt --check; cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features (no warnings);
cargo nextest run (within TESTING.md timeout); ratchets check.

title: Add impatiently_requeue_batches config flag; centralize split-requeue policy
description:
## Goal

Add a configuration flag `impatiently_requeue_batches: bool` (default `true`) that controls whether the scheduler re-queues batches on `pop()`. While doing this, refactor the split-requeue policy out of the inline body of `Scheduler::pop()` and into a single `&self` method on `Scheduler` so that all current and future policy reads at a glance.

## Background

The split-requeue feature was introduced in PR #150 and simplified in PR #183. Today, `Scheduler::pop()` re-queues every popped batch immediately:
- multi-test batches are split in half (two pushes), counter reset to 0
- single-test batches are re-queued with an incremented counter, capped at `MAX_SINGLE_TEST_REQUEUES` (3)

The user wants a single switch to disable BOTH behaviours (singleton hedge AND further halving). Name: `impatiently_requeue_batches` (the feature exists to hedge batches that might run long — hence "impatient").

## Required changes

### 1. Add config field
File: `src/config/schema.rs`, struct `OffloadConfig` (around line 47). Add:
```rust
/// When true, the scheduler re-queues each batch on pop:
/// multi-test batches are split into halves, and single-test batches
/// are re-queued up to MAX_SINGLE_TEST_REQUEUES times. This hedges against
/// batches whose runtime exceeds expectations. When false, batches run
/// exactly once on the sandbox that popped them.
#[serde(default = "default_impatiently_requeue_batches")]
pub impatiently_requeue_batches: bool,
```
Add a `fn default_impatiently_requeue_batches() -> bool { true }` near the other default fns (~line 97).

### 2. Plumb through Scheduler::new
File: `src/orchestrator/scheduler.rs`, `Scheduler::new` (currently line 110). Add a final `bool` parameter named `impatiently_requeue_batches` and store it on the `Scheduler` struct.

### 3. Extract the policy method
Add a `&self` method, e.g. `fn enqueue_followups(&self, batch: &ScheduledBatch)`, that contains the ENTIRE current policy block (the multi-test halving and the singleton retry-counter logic). It must be a no-op when `self.impatiently_requeue_batches` is false. `pop()` calls this method once per popped batch, just before returning.

### 4. Update callers
- `src/orchestrator.rs` ~line 388: pass `self.config.offload.impatiently_requeue_batches`.
- `src/history/store.rs` ~lines 759, 763, 822, 857: existing tests — pass `true` to preserve current behavior.
- In-file tests in `scheduler.rs` (~lines 314, 332, 362, and the requeue tests at ~604, 645, 675): pass `true` so existing assertions continue to hold.

### 5. New test
Add a test in `scheduler.rs` confirming that with the flag set to `false`:
- popping a multi-test batch yields the batch and leaves the queue empty (no halves re-queued)
- popping a single-test batch leaves the queue empty (no retry)
Model it on the existing `test_pop_requeues_batch` / `test_pop_requeues_single_test_batch_as_clone` tests.

### 6. Config tests
`src/config/schema.rs` and `src/config.rs` contain TOML parse tests. Verify the new field defaults to `true` when omitted, and parses correctly when set.

## Definition of Done
- `cargo fmt --check` passes
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features` passes (no warnings)
- `cargo nextest run` passes within TESTING.md timeout
- `ratchets check` passes
- New test exercises both flag values

## Constraints
- Minimal diff. Do NOT rename existing constants, restructure unrelated code, or drive-by refactor.
- The new policy method must be `&self` (not `&mut self`) — the scheduler is shared across workers via `Arc` and uses interior mutability via `Mutex`/`Notify`.
- Do NOT introduce a separate policy struct; a single method on `Scheduler` is the agreed shape.
- Preserve all existing public API behavior for the default (`true`) case.

title: apply-diff: handle directory-to-file transitions in write_patched_file
description: In write_patched_file (src/main.rs:1271), before calling std::fs::write, check if the target path is an existing directory and remove it with remove_dir_all. This handles the case where a git diff contains a Create operation for a file path that is currently a directory in the base image (e.g. .claude/skills/execute-implementation-plan was a directory at checkpoint 217120ea but became a file at MR SHA dfa1175d). Without this fix, std::fs::write fails with EISDIR. Add a test for this edge case.

title: Add AddAssign/Sum impl for CostEstimate to deduplicate fold pattern
description:
## Problem

The 2-field aggregation of `CostEstimate` (cpu_seconds, estimated_cost_usd) is now duplicated verbatim in at least three sites:

- `src/orchestrator.rs:694-701` (in the new `spawn_sandbox_cleanup` helper, code-99)
- `src/provider.rs:516-520` (test `cost_estimate_aggregation`)
- `src/provider.rs:529-533` (test `cost_estimate_aggregation_empty`)

Each callsite open-codes the same fold:

```rust
.fold(CostEstimate::default(), |mut acc, cost| {
    acc.cpu_seconds += cost.cpu_seconds;
    acc.estimated_cost_usd += cost.estimated_cost_usd;
    acc
})
```

Every new field added to `CostEstimate` would have to be added at all of these sites. The code-99 implementation added the third copy without flagging this.

## Required Changes

In `src/provider.rs`:
- Implement `std::ops::AddAssign<&CostEstimate>` (or `Add`) for `CostEstimate`.
- Implement `std::iter::Sum<&CostEstimate>` (or `Sum<CostEstimate>`) so call sites can write `costs.iter().sum::<CostEstimate>()`.
- Keep `Default` (which already gives zero); `Sum` uses `Default::default()` as the identity.

Then replace the three folds:
- `src/orchestrator.rs:694-701` -> `sandboxes.iter().map(|sb| sb.cost_estimate()).sum()`
- `src/provider.rs:516-520` and `:529-533` -> same.

## Acceptance criteria

- No remaining manual `acc.cpu_seconds += ... ; acc.estimated_cost_usd += ...` folds in the tree.
- Existing aggregation tests still pass.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy`, `cargo nextest run` all pass.

## Why now

code-99 was just merged and added the third copy of this pattern. Extracting now prevents the next sandbox-related feature from adding a fourth copy.

comments.text: Closed by Coordinator on 2026-05-19. This bead was filed by the Tidy Agent while reviewing the uncommitted code-99 diff, which added a third CostEstimate-fold copy at orchestrator.rs:694-701. code-99 was subsequently cancelled and its changes were discarded, so the third copy never landed. The remaining duplication is only between two test-only folds in provider.rs:516-520 and 529-533 — not P1-worthy on its own. If a future change adds a third production-side aggregation, refile.
